posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
This is an area I really want to learn more about. It could be the future of a decent amount of our future Burgundy buying. So, I thought a consolidated thread for tasting notes would be fun and informative. Obviously, this thread is for both reds and whites.

I will start with a 2015 Michel Juillot Bourgogne Rouge. A few years ago, we had a fairly large party at our country club for a lot of friends and family for a special occasion. As a member of the Club’s wine club, I was able to purchase wines essentially at retail through the wine club and then pay a corkage charge (I think before corkage this was somewhere between $25 and $30 a bottle). This was the red and the white was a 2015 Moreau-Naudet Chablis. Both of these wines were wonderful and, of course, we purchased a decent amount more than was necessary for the event. So, the rest was kept by the club (as are other bottles bought through the wine club) and I have been slowly drinking through them as I have dinner there. Saturday night we drank the last bottle of this really elegant wine. Not a big wine, but very elegant with a lot of complexity for a Bourgogne Rouge. Really have loved this wine and am sorry to see our bottles of this finished. [Still have a few of the wonderful Chablis. ]. Need to buy more wines from Juillot from Weygandt Wines.

posts.name: Phil T r o t t i e r
posts.cooked:
2018 Dureuil-Janthial Rully Rouge & 2018 Dureuil-Janthial Rully Blanc: Dureuil-Janthial Rully 2018 red & white - WINE TALK - WineBerserkers

2018 A. et P. de Villaine Bourgogne Côte Chalonnaise La Digoine: TN: 2018 A. et P. de Villaine Bourgogne Côte Chalonnaise La Digoine - WINE TALK - WineBerserkers

2017 Vignerons de Buxy Givry 1er Cru Clos Jus: Community Tasting Note - 2017 Vignerons de Buxy Givry 1er Cru Clos Jus - CellarTracker - Not bad. Nose of cranberry, cherry, herbs and a some hints of spices. The palate is enjoyable and still on the same fruits with fine tannins. Acidity is sufficient. One thing that doesn’t resonate with me though is this feeling that the fruits are a little “candied”. There is a perceived “sweetness” on the palate that hurts the harmony in this wine. As I was drinking this, I remained focused on that aspect… too bad.

Will you start another thread for the Mâconnais?

posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked:
commercial post:

I have imported the wines of Domaine Jacqueson (Paul & Marie Jacqueson) for over 30 years. Obviously I think they are very good. Not easy to find and not inexpensive.

Dan Kravitz

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
This is a region that’s very close to my heart, and which I’ve made a point to cover annually in TWA, along with all the “lesser known” AOCs of Burgundy, most of which suffer from the same vicious cycle of negative consumer perceptions that impose low prices, encouraging producers to privilege quantity and cash flow over quality, resulting in mediocre wines that reinforce those negative perceptions (I am looking at a book from the 19th century that laments Mercurey’s lost reputation, describing precisely this vicious cycle). There are more and more producers making very decent wine for a good price in the Côte Chalonnaise, but what excites me more are the handful that are going even further, trying to break that vicious cycle and make wines that prove that the limitations under which “lesser” AOCs operate are as much imposed as revealed. I would put Dureuil-Janthial and Bruno Lorenzon at the top of that list, as anyone who follows my work will know, but others e.g. François Lumpp (one of the first to plant high quality massale selections taken from the Côte d’Or in the Côte Chalonnaise) have been important too.

One producer folks might not have heard of is Alain Hasard of Domaine Les Champs de l’Abbaye. He does everything right (massale selections, high density, organic, hand harvesting at maturity, classical winemaking with well-chosen barrels and long élevage: the things that make great e.g Givry just as surely as they make great Gevrey!) and his wines are very sensual and charming—and age well, too. He’s the only estate-bottler in the village of Aluze, which has some top sites that are “only” entitled to Bourgogne Côte Chalonnaise AOC, but which are much more interesting from an agronomic perspective than most Bourgogne on the plain in front of the Côte d’Or (sloping clay-limestone soils with prime exposure and drainage, rather than flat, endless mud). One could make the case that Aluze merits an AOC of its own just as much as Bouzeron. Anyway, the wines were a revelation to me and I highly recommend them.

Looking at the younger generation, there is plenty of promise in the region, but two new arrivals who are definitely following through so far are Gautier Desvignes at Domaine Desvignes in Givry and Maxime Cottenceau in Buxy (a Dureuil alumnus, most of his vineyards are in Montagny).

The Côte Chalonnaise is also a very beautiful place to spend time, with a couple of decent rustic restaurants, so it’s definitely worth a day trip if anyone is ever in Burgundy. It is quite easy to get a handle, at least superficially, on the Côte d’Or (south-east facing limestone escarpment, check). But the Côte Chalonnaise is quite complicated: Rully, for example, has a first hillside in front of the village; and a second overlooking the village; which in turn becomes a valley leading up to a plateau (largely hidden from view) above the village. Much generic négociant Rully comes from machine harvested fruit grown on this plateau. Meix Cadot, for example, sits on the first and warmest slope in front of the village; and Chapître, to take another, is in the heart of the second slope overlooking the village, a real sun trap; whereas e.g. Grésigny is towards the end of the valley that winds round up to the plateau, with great exposition but a much cooler mesoclimate. Figuring out stuff like this helps make sense of why Meix Cadot has such weight; why Chapître is so fleshy and precocious; and why Grésigny is so tensile. But it is not easy to find resources that explain all of this. So visiting bears dividends.

If anyone has any questions they want to ask about the region, I’d be happy to try to field them to the best of my abilities!

Looking at the younger generation, there is plenty of promise in the region, but two new arrivals who are definitely following through so far are Gautier Desvignes at Domaine Desvignes in Givry and Maxime Cottenceau in Buxy (a Dureuil alumnus, most of his vineyards are in Montagny).

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Has anyone tasted a red or white Rully from Domaine de la Folie?

Domaine de la Folie was historically one of, if not the, top estate in Rully (especially for still wines: historically, Rully got rich in the 19th century making crémant). It was owned by one of the pioneers of photography and was the place to be back in the day. It’s to the north of the AOC, surrounded by woods, in a somewhat cooler mesoclimate but with fine expositions. Clémence and Baptiste Dubrulle are the owners today, and they’re making progress, step by step, in restoring its reputation. They’re very nice wines, and they also make one of the best Marc de Bourgogne that you can find (and it is not expensive). In white, the Clos St Jacques is the top wine, in red, the Cuvée Marey. The Aligoté is from great raw materials and I’m encouraging them to vinify it in used barrels because it could definitely benefit from it.

posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked:
Agree with William, this was a top Domaine that went downhill. I haven’t had the wines lately, but glad to hear they are on the mend.

Dan Kravitz

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
We sold Domaine de la Folie back in the '70s, when one of our customers partnered w Becky Wasserman to bring in unknown Burgundies. I posted our 1978 catalog on Instagram.
$7.25 for the Clos St Jacques.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: Let us assume that someday in the future (2022???) I will be able to travel again and go to Burgundy. Are there Caveaus or something like that in the Chalonnaise that are good where one can go in and taste a range of these producers?

I have had Rully rouge from Chanzy and Claudie Jobard that I have liked (both 2014 vintage). Good wines and I don’t think I paid more than $25 for them at the time.
I also purchased several vintages of Givry 1ers from Domaine X. Besson and Domaine Joblot, and Mercurey village from Faiveley.

New for me and haven’t tried yet, on Mr. Kelley’s recommendation, I have bought 2017 & 2018 of Bruno Lorenzon’s Mercurey Le Chapitre.

The other new one for me is the 2019 Jaeger-Defaix Rully 1er Les Cloux blanc. Their reds are next!

Note: I have in the past or currently have sold these wines in my retail position, but I am only posting wines that I have also purchased for my own cellar.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Let us assume that someday in the future (2022???) I will be able to travel again and go to Burgundy. Are there Caveaus or something like that in the Chalonnaise that are good where one can go in and taste a range of these producers?

I think you’d be better served just visiting one or two of the top producers. The best are much more interesting, and instructive, than the average. Folks tend to be very generous with their time. Hey, if I have a morning free I’ll give you a guided tour

I’ve visited a lot of Cote Chalonnaise producers, both as a professional and as a consumer. In general, it’s far more easy-going than the Cote d’Or.

Dan Kravitz

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: Had a Bruno Lorenzon Mercurey 1er Cru Champs Martin Cuvée Carline 2017 the other night. My first wine from Lorenzon and I was excited to try it. Had a lot of rich, beautiful fruit. Tasted like a 2017 from the Cote d’Or in that it was very accessible and had nice acidity making the wine taste very fresh. Not that complex at the moment, but that could well come with age. Very enjoyable.

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked: It’s been a decade but there was a very good caviste in Bouzeron selling all the best Domaines at their cellar door prices.

posts.name: Phil T r o t t i e r
posts.cooked:
Domaine Faiveley Mercurey la Framboisière Monopole 2019

I’ve purchased five different Faiveley Mercureys in 2019 but have none in hand yet. I have lots of experience from previous vintages so I’m pretty confident in them. Really looking forward to trying them.

Popped one open tonight. There is nothing shy about this: nose of sweet berries (cherry, raspberry), spices, florals, a little bit of “rafle” and some oak as it warms up. Beautiful bouquet. The palate is pretty much in line with the nose: plenty of fruit, good concentration and complexity, nice acidity, powdery tanins and a hint of heat on the finish. This is serious but the fruit and acidity makes it accessible right now. This should age very nicely. Great vintage for this cuvée. Great QPR.

Half a bottle left under Repour. I will revisit tomorrow.

Edit day 2: No worry. Day 1 impressions hold.
